# Questions in Livestream 167

Live Stream #167: Super Expensive Egyptian Ritual Practice

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:14</a> | If Adam and Eve were special priestly creations rather than the first humans biologically, would that break the redemption narrative? | He says no and still treats Adam as the key covenantal figure through whom the problem of sin is framed. | He argues that Romans frames sin as entering through "one man," not necessarily through the first biological human. On the priestly-creation reading, Adam functions as the representative or federal head of humanity, so his sin brings consequences on those he represents, just as a ruler's decision can affect a whole nation. He says that this does not damage the redemption narrative, because Christ as the second Adam likewise represents and redeems humanity under that same representative logic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:07</a> | Does the Ipuwer Papyrus confirm the ten plagues? | He says no and treats the comparisons as loose analogies rather than direct confirmation. | He places the Ipuwer Papyrus in the Twelfth Dynasty and says its language and content fit Middle Kingdom political literature, not the Exodus event. He acknowledges that some details, such as the river becoming bloody or undrinkable, resemble plague traditions, but he treats those as Egyptian crisis motifs tied to earlier disruptions, inundation problems, and political propaganda. In his summary, the papyrus does not confirm the Exodus plagues, though it shows that Egypt had remembered events that could sound similar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:19</a> | Did ancient Egyptian cities have parks? | He says no in the modern public-park sense and explains that Egyptian urban planning worked differently. | He says Egyptian cities were crowded urban spaces and did not have public parks as a civic institution. The closest analogues were gardens and zoos attached to temples or elite religious estates, and those were restricted to priests and upper-status participants rather than open recreational spaces. Their purpose was religious display and experience connected to a local deity, not casual public leisure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=1238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:38</a> | What should people make of the Emerald Tablets of Thoth, reformed Egyptian, and similar claims? | He treats them as spurious modern religious claims rather than genuine ancient Egyptian traditions. | He groups these as examples of new religious movements producing special authoritative writings. For the Emerald Tablets, he says there is no evidence for the claimed discovery at Giza and describes the content as a modern mix of theosophical and Lovecraft-like ideas rather than ancient Egyptian tradition. For the Book of Abraham, he says Joseph Smith used an Egyptian papyrus as a channeled text, but the surviving copies show material from the Book of the Dead rather than the claimed Abrahamic translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=1616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:56</a> | What material would he recommend for a father doing Bible study with children ages seven to thirteen? | He recommends a good children's Bible for younger children, a regular Bible for older children, and a parent-led study that lets the children ask questions. | He recommends using a strong children's Bible for the younger children, especially one that paraphrases biblical narratives clearly without hiding the seriousness of the stories. Around age ten and up, he says children should begin moving into a regular Bible. The study should be a family Bible study led by the father, with the children invited to ask questions and the parent taking responsibility to look up answers and connect Scripture to life in a fallen world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=1873" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:13</a> | Is the common picture of heaven as endless spouse-less work basically a capitalist caricature? | He says no and argues that work itself is not a curse and heaven should not be reduced to that caricature. | He rejects the idea that work belongs only to capitalism or to the curse, saying that work is part of creation before the fall. Adam's tending of the garden was pre-fall; the curse is the frustration of work through thorns, thistles, and resistance, not work itself. He says work can be pleasurable and creative, and he imagines eternal life as involving productive good works for one another rather than idle inactivity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=2179" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:19</a> | What is the best way to learn ancient Hebrew without institutional training? | He says the best option is to take an introductory Hebrew course online, because self-teaching is very difficult. | He says learning Hebrew without formal help is very difficult, so the best practical substitute is an online introductory Hebrew course. The crucial early hurdle is mastering the alphabet, the consonants, and the Masoretic vowel points. He says many students drop out in the first month because the writing system is unfamiliar, but once those foundations are learned the rest becomes more manageable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=2280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:00</a> | How many letters does Hebrew have? | He says he thinks it has about twenty-two letters, while noting that the vowel pointing makes it more complicated. | He answers cautiously that Hebrew has about twenty-two letters and says he may be remembering the number imperfectly. The consonants are not the main difficulty, in his view; the harder part is the Masoretic pointing and vowel system. He also notes that some consonants, such as aleph and ayin, involve sounds unfamiliar to English speakers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=2484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:24</a> | Was Eglon's palace at Jericho, as Bryant Wood claims? | He says the text points to Eglon ruling from Jericho, while the precise archaeology and location claims need careful handling. | He says Judges reports Eglon capturing the "city of palms," which he identifies as Jericho, but the text does not say Eglon ruled from Jericho or that his palace was there. He describes Bryant Wood's argument as tied to redating Jericho's destruction around 1400 BC for an early Exodus model, but he thinks the archaeological case is strained. He notes later work indicating the city was rebuilt after the Middle Bronze destruction and says the proposed palace argument does not really prove Ehud's date or Eglon's residence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=2913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:33</a> | What commentaries does he recommend on the Pentateuch as a whole or on its individual books? | He gives resource guidance and emphasizes using serious book-specific commentaries rather than assuming one volume can do all the work. | He points people toward curated commentary lists, such as the Gospel Coalition resource he mentions, because the right commentary depends on the user's purpose. He distinguishes lay, preaching, teaching, and exegetical commentaries and says no single recommendation fits every need. He strongly encourages using multiple current, serious commentaries, even for preaching, because they can correct assumptions and expose better readings of difficult texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=3573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:33</a> | Is he familiar with the criticisms Preston Sprinkle received from Rosaria Butterfield, and what are his thoughts? | He gives a limited answer, distinguishing what he knows from what he has not followed closely. | He says he is not aware of the specific comments from Rosaria Butterfield toward Preston Sprinkle, though he can guess the general topic from what he knows of Sprinkle. Because the issue appears pastorally sensitive and had come up repeatedly, he asks the questioner to take the matter offline through his contact information rather than trying to address it on the YouTube livestream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=3724" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:04</a> | What resources can help with ancient Near Eastern Old Testament context for New Testament understandings of the Trinity? | He says the topic is very specific and suggests targeted research rather than an easy single-source answer. | He says he does not know a ready resource for that exact combination of topics. Work exists on the Old Testament and the Trinity, and there may be work on Old Testament background for sacraments, but he expects the more specific ancient Near Eastern angle to require journal research. He recommends a theological library or seminary library and says the question likely calls for a deeper academic search rather than one easy book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=3913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:13</a> | Who was the Assyrian high god? | He says the answer is not simple but identifies Asshur as central in Assyrian religion. | He says there is no single simple "top god" answer because Mesopotamia was not a monolithic religious culture. Different city-states and periods elevated different deities, such as Marduk, Asshur, Ninurta, Nanna, and Enlil. For Assyria specifically, Asshur was a chief deity connected to the city of Asshur and Assyrian identity, but the broader Mesopotamian world had multiple high-status gods rather than one uniform hierarchy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=4095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:15</a> | Is "Ur of the Chaldeans" in Genesis 15:7 a lexical gloss? | He checks the passage and treats the wording as a plausible explanatory place-name issue rather than a simple anachronism. | He says some commentators do treat "of the Chaldeans" as a gloss that clarifies which Ur is meant, but he personally does not think that is the best explanation. He suggests it may be a scribal or transcriptional updating, where an earlier form resembling a different place-name was changed by a copyist into the familiar "Chaldeans." He emphasizes that this is his view and may be a minority position. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=4311" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:51</a> | What is the importance of the donkey never having been ridden in Mark 11:2? | He says the unused animal has cultic implications because it has not been ritually defiled by ordinary use. | He explains the unridden colt as an animal set apart for sacred use. Because no one had sat on it, pulled a load with it, or used it in ordinary labor, it carried no ritual defilement from common use. He compares this to the Old Testament example of the milk cows used to return the ark, which had never pulled a load, and connects the detail to ritual purity in the Messiah's entrance into Jerusalem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=4788" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:48</a> | Why does Luke say Heli was Joseph's father and not Mary's? | He explains the genealogy as functioning through Joseph legally while still addressing the maternal-line interpretation. | He says the repeated phrase "son of" is largely supplied by translators in Luke's genealogy, so the wording does not necessarily require a strict father-to-son chain at every point. He interprets Luke as giving a maternal line expressed through Joseph as legal proxy, comparable to cases where a daughter's husband carries a family line when there are no sons. On that reading, Heli is Mary's father, while Joseph is named because the genealogy is represented through him legally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=5037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:57</a> | How much would it cost to fund commentaries for Exodus, Numbers, and Chronicles? | He says serious commentary projects are expensive because they require sustained scholarly time and support. | He says a commentary can take roughly half a year if it is the main task, but given his existing content and writing schedule it could take a full year. He compares the cost to prior book-funding estimates and says a theoretical commentary project would require replacing the income from work he would have to set aside. He suggests something in the range of roughly $20,000 to $25,000, while noting that fellowships for book projects can run higher. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=5750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:50</a> | What ancient law codes would Abraham have known or quoted? | He says very old law traditions are in play here, so parallels with early law collections are not a defeater for Abrahamic antiquity. | He says the oldest law code Abraham appears to quote is the Code of Lipit-Ishtar, around 1930 BC, while Hammurabi is late eighteenth century BC and seems to be the latest relevant law code. He frames the parallels as fitting a roughly 1930 to 1725 BC window. In his view, that range challenges both very early and very late dates for Abraham, because the legal parallels cluster in a specific Middle Bronze context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=6350" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:50</a> | Was Israel's conquest of the promised land genocide, as some scholars suggest? | He says no, reading the conquest language as ancient hyperbolic warfare language rather than modern genocide language. | He says it was not genocide, partly because the destruction was limited to a few strategic cities such as Jericho, Ai, and Hazor rather than an entire people. He stresses that the land was already under pressure from Amorites, Egyptians, Philistines, and other powers, so Israel was entering a contested geopolitical situation rather than simply erasing a settled population. He also argues that the long-term conquest involved centuries of settlement, loss of external support for garrison cities, intermarriage, and cultural exchange, not only sword warfare. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=7368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:48</a> | How should the geography of Uz and the Sabeans in Job 1:13-17 be understood? | He treats the passage as geographically difficult but interpretable in ancient Near Eastern terms. | He places Uz in the Aram or Syria region, a crossroads between northern Mesopotamia and Arabia. The Chaldeans represent a northern Mesopotamian threat, while the Sabeans represent migratory Arabian tribes moving through routes east of the Jordan. The geography makes Job trapped between attacks from north and south, with the named peoples functioning as compass extremes around him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=8170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:10</a> | Why does "The Rain in Spain" joke that Hebrews learn their language backwards? | He explains it as a joke about Hebrew being read right to left, not as a serious linguistic claim. | He says the line is probably just referring to Hebrew reading direction. English is read left to right, while Hebrew is read right to left, and Hebrew books open in the reverse direction from English books. He compares it to the reading direction familiar from manga and notes that some ancient scripts, such as Egyptian, can be arranged in more than one direction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=9651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:51</a> | In Jeremiah 13:23, why does the text mention the Kushite's skin and the leopard's spots? | He says it is a rhetorical question using things that do not change to make a moral point. | He says the original wording is "Kushite," and the verse uses the Kushite's skin and the leopard's spots as examples of things that cannot naturally be changed. The point is rhetorical: if those fixed traits cannot change, then the audience, accustomed to evil, is being confronted with the difficulty of doing good. He calls the examples somewhat random but fitting because they represent inherent, recognizable attributes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=9878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:38</a> | Where can people find ancient Near Eastern maps they can edit and use publicly? | He says ready-made options are limited and leans toward building or commissioning maps instead of assuming easy copyright-free sources. | He says good ready-made maps are usually copyrighted, so the safer path is to make your own. His recommendation is to start from public-domain NASA satellite imagery, save the source documentation, and then build the map yourself in a vector program such as Adobe Illustrator. He cautions that Google imagery is licensed and not copyright-free, and mentions Frutiger as a useful map font because it remains legible at small sizes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=10134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:54</a> | How should someone respond to a pastor claiming to discern which songs are or are not inspired by the Holy Spirit? | He urges caution and grounds discernment in theology rather than a pastor's unsupported impressions. | He says judging songs by a pastor's personal feeling of what is or is not from the Holy Spirit is a poor method. Discernment should be theological: compare the song or claim to the Word of God. If it measures up to Scripture, it may be from the Spirit; if it does not, it probably is not, though something not being inspired does not automatically make it bad. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=10246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:46</a> | What are his thoughts on Billy Carson? | He strongly criticizes Carson and treats his claims as conspiracy-driven rather than scholarly. | He dismisses Billy Carson as a purveyor of conspiracy and fringe theories rather than a serious theologian or historian. He says Carson seems to embrace almost any conspiracy theory and expresses surprise that people treat him as a credible source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=10343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:23</a> | Is Isaiah 7:14 future prophecy, about Isaiah's own time, or both? | He says it can be both, with an immediate setting and a fuller future manifestation. | He reads Isaiah 7:14 as both tied to Isaiah's immediate setting and recontextualized for a later messianic fulfillment. In the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, Ahaz is told to trust the Lord and receive the sign of Immanuel, but because he instead seeks Assyrian help, the immediate sign becomes Maher-shalal-hash-baz in Isaiah 8. He says the Immanuel prophecy still stands and later manifests in the Messiah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=10769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:29</a> | How should miracles noticed by foreign nations in 2 Kings 5:5 and 2 Chronicles 32:31 be understood? | He says foreign observers could notice Yahweh's acts, but the biblical point is not that surrounding nations fully understood them. | He distinguishes the two passages rather than treating them as one pattern. In 2 Kings 5, the king of Aram has heard enough about healings connected with Israel to send Naaman for help, paying a large sum for a valued servant's healing. In 2 Chronicles 32, the Babylonian envoys are more interested in Hezekiah's wealth and political value as Babylon seeks allies against Assyria; the result is that Babylon learns Israel has treasures worth plundering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=11640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:00</a> | Can spiritual beings communicate telepathically or read minds? | He says they may communicate in ways analogous to telepathy, but they cannot read minds. | He distinguishes transmission from reception. Spiritual beings may communicate by transmitting thoughts, since they do not have ordinary physical speech organs, but he says they cannot read another mind. They can observe physical tells, reactions, or changes in behavior and make guesses, but that is not the same as knowing the inner content of a person's thoughts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=12053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:53</a> | What are his thoughts on Orthodox saints and miracle claims from Mount Athos or monasteries? | He is cautious about miracle claims and does not accept them uncritically. | He says he evaluates miracle claims case by case rather than accepting or rejecting all of them in bulk. Likewise, he thinks some recognized saints were faithful believers while others were scoundrels, giving Cyril of Alexandria as an example of someone he considers both a brilliant theologian and a ruthless political actor. His practical answer is that saints and miracle stories should be judged individually. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=13320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:00</a> | Are there any good Bible comics? | He says he does not know of good examples and is skeptical of the Bible manga he has seen. | He says he does not know of good Bible comics and is more familiar with manga than Western comics. The Bible manga he has seen strikes him as poor, though he leaves open that the concept could be done well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/GRa11_37Vy8?t=13636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:16</a> | What was the Tower of Babel built for, and how would it prevent people from being scattered? | He says the tower represents an urban centralizing project and a human attempt to resist dispersion. | He says Babel was an urban and religious centralization project meant to keep humanity unified and famous rather than scattered. The tower is not mainly about physically reaching heaven, but about making appeals to heaven, making a name, and binding people together around a shared religious-political center. He says the alarming part of Genesis 11 is not the size of the ziggurat but the unified human purpose behind it, especially with one language enabling coordinated action. |
